source.title: Neural computations underlying arbitration between model-based and model-free learning

confidence_rationale: Empirical confirmation of the Daw 2005 normative arbitration framework. Human fMRI shows inferior lateral PFC and frontopolar cortex track per-channel reliability signals AND the comparison between them, with effective connectivity to model-free valuation regions modulated by the arbitrator's verdict. Direct R4 anchor for MECH-312's neural substrate, distinct from but complementary to Daw 2005.
failure_signatures: If REE's MECH-312 arbitration substrate does not produce reliability signals for each channel (or does not compare them), the Lee 2014 dissociation pattern (lateral PFC tracks reliability comparison; effective connectivity to MF valuation modulated accordingly) is unrecoverable.

If the arbitration mechanism is implemented as a single hardcoded threshold or schedule rather than as a per-channel reliability comparison, the Lee 2014 PFC-modulation signature will be absent and the substrate will not behave like the human arbitrator.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Human fMRI during a Markov-decision task shows that inferior lateral prefrontal cortex and frontopolar cortex encode (i) reliability signals for each of the model-based and model-free systems and (ii) the output of a comparison between those reliability signals. Effective connectivity from these prefrontal regions to model-free valuation areas is negatively modulated by the degree of model-based control, suggesting arbitration may operate via prefrontal modulation of the model-free system rather than via a separate downstream gating circuit.
mapping.ree_translation: MECH-312's arbitration substrate should track per-channel reliability and a comparison signal -- analogous to the lateral PFC / frontopolar role Lee 2014 identifies. The implementation mechanism (modulating the dominant channel's downstream output rather than competing-output selection) is also informative: REE's MECH-312 design should consider implementing arbitration as a modulation on an always-running channel rather than as a switch between channels.
mapping.mapping_caveat: Lee 2014 specifically tests MB-vs-MF arbitration in a two-step Markov decision task. ARC-062 / ARC-064 / ARC-065 are not strict MB/MF analogs (different axis). The neural substrate (lateral PFC reliability comparison) is plausibly general but the specific contrast Lee tests (MB-MF) may map onto only a subset of MECH-312's arbitration scope. Transfer risk moderate.
mapping.source_context: Neuron empirical fMRI paper. Caltech (Lee, Shimojo, O'Doherty). Tested 22 healthy adults on a sequential decision task. Cited >800 times.

confidence_components.notes: Neuron venue + clean fMRI methodology. Mapping fidelity moderate-high for the lateral-PFC-as-arbitrator claim. Transfer risk slightly elevated because the MB-vs-MF axis is not a strict analog of REE's top-down-vs-bottom-up rule axis.
